profile:
Theo Davenport
Head of AI at Clauselink

I came to AI through a fairly traditional NLP research path. I did my PhD at Cascadia State University on information extraction from semi-structured documents, then joined Helix Labs in Westchester for three years, where I worked on entity resolution pipelines for financial regulatory filings. The work was rigorous and I learned a lot about what production NLP actually requires — namely, that precision matters more than recall when the downstream consumer is a human who has to act on the output.

From IBM I moved to a Series B startup called Contegra, which built compliance automation tooling for insurance carriers. I led the NLP team there, growing it from two engineers to eight over two years. We built a clause-level extraction system on top of BERT-era models, and I made the call early to use ensemble methods rather than fine-tuning a single large model, mostly because our labeled data was thin and the ensembles were more auditable — something insurance clients cared about deeply. We got acquired by a larger RegTech firm in 2021, and I stayed through the integration for about eight months before deciding the pace wasn't right for me.

After Contegra I spent eighteen months at Verdigris AI as a member of technical staff focused on document understanding applications, which is where I got deep exposure to the current generation of large language models and their failure modes in structured extraction tasks. I worked closely with the evals team to build benchmarks for multi-page document comprehension, and that experience shaped how I think about measuring AI quality in domains where ground truth is genuinely ambiguous — something that comes up constantly in legal text.

I joined Clauselink about two years ago because the contract review problem is one of the hardest document AI problems I know of. Legal language is adversarially ambiguous by design. My team currently has four ML engineers, two research engineers, and a data annotation lead. We run a hybrid architecture: a retrieval-augmented pipeline handles clause identification and cross-reference resolution, while a fine-tuned LLM layer handles risk classification and deviation flagging against customer-specific playbooks. The hardest ongoing challenge is maintaining accuracy as customer playbooks diverge — we've been investing heavily in per-customer adapter layers rather than full model retraining, which has meaningfully reduced our update latency.

My current focus is on two things: getting our confidence calibration to a point where the product can surface reliable uncertainty signals to reviewers, and expanding coverage from MSAs and NDAs into more bespoke agreement types like JVs and IP licensing. Both require better training data than we have, so a non-trivial part of my time goes into annotation infrastructure and quality control.

Outside of Clauselink, I advise two early-stage startups in the document intelligence space, I contribute intermittently to the open-source RetrieveStack project, and I speak at legal tech conferences a few times a year, mostly on the gap between benchmark performance and real-world production behavior in legal AI systems.
company: Clauselink is a B2B SaaS company focused on AI-powered contract review for enterprise legal and procurement teams. The platform enables legal professionals to extract, classify, and flag risk across commercial agreements at scale, with support for customer-specific playbooks that reflect individual negotiation standards. Clauselink's AI layer combines retrieval-augmented extraction with fine-tuned classification models, providing reviewers with structured risk summaries and deviation alerts rather than raw model output. The company serves mid-market and enterprise customers in sectors including technology, financial services, and professional services. Clauselink has raised a Series C round and is expanding its coverage beyond standard agreement types into more complex deal structures.

profile:
Tara Krishnamurthy
Founder & CEO at Mindbridge Health

I came to this problem from the wrong side of it. Before starting Mindbridge, I spent eight years in employee benefits consulting at Mercer, where I watched companies pour budget into EAP contracts that almost nobody used. The utilization rates were embarrassing — typically three to five percent — and the employers had no visibility into why, and employees had no real reason to trust a service their company had procured for them. That frustration is the founding thesis of everything we have built.

I am not a technical founder. My background is in organizational psychology and benefits strategy, and I want to be clear about that because I think it shapes how we approach the product. I hire for depth in the engineering and clinical science functions, and I try to stay out of their way on implementation decisions. What I do bring is an unusually detailed understanding of how HR buyers think, what procurement cycles look like inside a mid-market company, and what employees actually need to feel safe using a mental health tool their employer is paying for.

Mindbridge operates on two surfaces simultaneously. On the consumer side, we have a self-guided app that individuals can download, access anonymously, and use for mood tracking, guided CBT exercises, and on-demand text support from licensed therapists. On the employer side, we sell a SaaS dashboard that gives HR and benefits teams aggregate, anonymized population-level insights — utilization trends, engagement cohorts, early signals of workforce stress — without ever exposing individual data. The privacy architecture was the hardest design problem we faced in the first twelve months, and we went through three iterations before we had something that the clinical team, the legal team, and the product team could all live with.

We closed our Series A last year and used most of it to build out the AI layer on both surfaces. On the consumer side, we now have an intake and session-routing model that triages incoming users based on their self-reported history and presenting concerns, and routes them either to self-guided content, asynchronous text therapy, or a recommendation to seek higher-acuity care outside the platform. The model was trained in close collaboration with our clinical advisory board, and we have a human review loop that audits a sample of routing decisions every week. On the employer side, we added an AI-generated commentary layer to the analytics dashboard — natural language summaries that translate the population data into plain language recommendations for HR teams who do not have a data analyst on staff.

I spend a lot of my time now on the enterprise sales motion, which we are still figuring out. Mid-market employers with two hundred to two thousand employees are the sweet spot, and the sales cycle is longer than I expected when we started. I also sit on the advisory board of two other digital health companies and try to get to at least two benefits-and-wellbeing industry conferences a year.

The thing I keep coming back to is trust. Both the employee using the app and the employer buying the platform have to believe the incentives are aligned. Getting that architecture right — technically, legally, and in terms of how we talk about it — is the work I find most interesting.
company: Mindbridge Health is a hybrid B2C/B2B mental health platform serving individual users and their employers through two integrated product surfaces. The consumer app provides anonymous access to mood tracking, CBT-based self-guided programs, and on-demand licensed therapist support. The employer-facing SaaS layer delivers anonymized, population-level workforce wellbeing analytics and AI-generated insight summaries to HR and benefits teams. Founded by a benefits strategy and organizational psychology professional, Mindbridge has built its clinical and privacy architecture around a strict separation of individual and aggregate data. The company raised a Series A and has since deployed AI-driven triage and routing on the consumer side, alongside a natural language analytics layer for enterprise clients. Its primary market is mid-market employers in North America across professional services, technology, and healthcare verticals.

profile:
Andreas Hartmann
Mitgründer & CTO bei Lucenta GmbH (Series B, ~130 Mitarbeiter)

Ich habe Lucenta 2018 zusammen mit meiner Co-Gründerin gegründet, nachdem ich fast ein Jahrzehnt lang als Softwarearchitekt und später als Engineering Lead in der Unternehmensberatung gearbeitet hatte. Mein Hintergrund liegt in der Informatik — ich habe in Karlsruhe studiert und anschließend bei einem mittelständischen ERP-Anbieter in Frankfurt als Entwickler angefangen, bevor ich zu einer Berliner Beratungsfirma gewechselt bin, die Finanzprozesse für produzierende Unternehmen digitalisiert hat. Dort habe ich gelernt, wie tief die Ineffizienzen im deutschen Mittelstand wirklich sitzen — und wie wenig die damals verfügbaren Softwarelösungen daran geändert haben.

Lucenta baut eine ERP-Plattform speziell für mittelständische Unternehmen mit 50 bis 500 Mitarbeitern. Der Kern unseres Produkts ist ein KI-gestütztes Buchhaltungsmodul, das wir ursprünglich als Ergänzung zu unserem Basisprodukt entwickelt haben, das sich aber schnell zum zentralen Differenzierungsmerkmal entwickelt hat. Das Modul erkennt Belegmuster, kategorisiert Transaktionen automatisch, schlägt Kontozuordnungen vor und markiert Abweichungen, die menschliche Prüfung erfordern. Wir setzen dabei auf ein Ensemble aus klassischen ML-Modellen und einem feinabgestimmten Sprachmodell, das auf deutschsprachige Finanzdokumente spezialisiert ist. Die Genauigkeit bei der automatischen Buchung liegt bei unseren Bestandskunden inzwischen bei über 91 Prozent.

Technisch verantworte ich nach wie vor die gesamte Plattformarchitektur. Wir haben früh auf eine service-orientierte Architektur gesetzt, die wir schrittweise in Richtung Event-Driven-Muster weiterentwickelt haben — insbesondere dort, wo die KI-Komponenten asynchron mit dem Kern kommunizieren müssen. Das hat uns Flexibilität bei der Iteration der Modelle gegeben, ohne den Produktkern zu destabilisieren. Als wir in die Series B gegangen sind, haben wir außerdem begonnen, unsere Daten-Infrastruktur ernsthafter auszubauen: ein eigenes Feature-Store-System, Monitoring für Modell-Drift, und ein internes Evaluierungs-Framework, das neue Modellversionen automatisch gegen unsere Kundendaten-Benchmarks prüft, bevor sie in Produktion gehen.

Das Engineering-Team besteht heute aus 38 Personen, aufgeteilt in vier Product-Engineering-Teams, ein Platform-Team und ein dediziertes AI/ML-Team mit sieben Personen. Ich arbeite eng mit unserer Head of AI zusammen, die ich 2022 von einem Forschungsinstitut in München abgeworben habe. Gemeinsam legen wir die technische Roadmap für die nächsten 18 Monate fest, die neben dem Ausbau der Buchhaltungs-KI auch ein neues Modul für Liquiditätsplanung mit prädiktiven Komponenten umfasst.

Neben meiner Arbeit bei Lucenta bin ich gelegentlich als technischer Advisor für frühe B2B-SaaS-Startups tätig, halte Vorträge auf der DOAG und der KI-Konferenz München und habe kleinere Open-Source-Beiträge zu deutschen NLP-Preprocessing-Bibliotheken geleistet. Ich glaube, dass Software für den Mittelstand nicht auf Kosten der Benutzerfreundlichkeit komplex sein muss — und dass KI dann ihren größten Mehrwert liefert, wenn sie still im Hintergrund arbeitet, anstatt sich in den Vordergrund zu drängen.
company: Lucenta GmbH ist ein B2B-SaaS-Unternehmen mit Sitz in Frankfurt am Main, das eine ERP-Plattform für den deutschen und deutschsprachigen Mittelstand entwickelt. Das Kernprodukt adressiert Finanz- und Buchhaltungsprozesse für produzierende und handelsgetriebene Unternehmen mit 50 bis 500 Mitarbeitern. Das Differenzierungsmerkmal von Lucenta ist ein KI-gestütztes Buchhaltungsmodul, das Belegerkennung, automatische Transaktionskategorisierung und Anomalie-Markierung kombiniert. Das Unternehmen befindet sich in der Series B, beschäftigt rund 130 Mitarbeiter und bedient mehrere hundert Unternehmenskunden in der DACH-Region. Lucenta wurde 2018 gegründet und wird von europäischen Wachstumsinvestoren finanziert.

I started Attribus about three years ago after spending a long time watching marketing teams make budget decisions based on data they didn't fully trust. The core problem we kept running into was attribution — understanding which touchpoints actually moved a deal forward, not just which ones happened to be last in the chain.

We raised our Series A earlier this year, which brought us to around 40 people. Most of that growth has gone into the product and data teams.

The work I'm most focused on right now is our AI layer — we have a feature in active development that surfaces attribution anomalies automatically, so a marketing ops lead can see when a channel's performance signature changes before it shows up in a quarterly report. Before Attribus I worked across a few different roles touching analytics, go-to-market, and product decisions. I don't come from a single clean discipline, which I think is both a liability and an asset in this space.

profile:

Head of ML på Fastigra (Serie A, ~55 personer)

Jag leder maskininlärningsarbetet på Fastigra, där mitt team bygger de modeller som ligger till grund för vår energioptimeringsplatform. Innan jag kom till Fastigra tillbringade jag fyra år på ett konsultbolag inom energisektorn där jag byggde prognosmodeller för elnätsoperatörer, och dessförinnan jobbade jag med rekommendationssystem på en e-handelsplattform.
